questions.q: What is the primary benefit of composition over inheritance in object design?
options: Composition is always faster than inheritance
Composition provides greater flexibility by delegating to objects rather than inheriting rigid hierarchies
Composition requires less memory than inheritance
Composition allows classes to be compiled separately from their dependencies
questions.explanation: Composition is more flexible because behavior comes from contained objects that can be swapped at runtime, whereas inheritance creates a fixed hierarchy. This enables the Strategy pattern and other flexible designs.

questions.q: What does loose coupling refer to in object-oriented design?
options: Classes that perform no operations
Classes that depend minimally on each other's internal details
Classes that have very few methods
Classes that never inherit from other classes
questions.explanation: Loose coupling means classes depend on abstractions rather than concrete implementations, reducing the impact of changes. When a class only knows what it needs to use, it's loosely coupled.

questions.q: What is cohesion in a class or module?
options: The degree to which code is compressed or minified
The degree to which elements within a module belong together and serve a common purpose
The number of external libraries a class uses
The inheritance depth of a class hierarchy
questions.explanation: High cohesion means related functionality is grouped together in a single class, making it focused and easier to understand. Low cohesion indicates unrelated tasks mixed together.

questions.q: How do design by contract and abstraction relate?
options: They are completely unrelated concepts
Abstraction hides implementation details while contracts (interfaces) define what the abstraction promises to do
Abstraction requires contracts while contracts make abstraction impossible
Design by contract eliminates the need for abstraction
questions.explanation: Abstraction defines what something does (its public interface), while contracts specify the expected behavior and guarantees. Together they enable code to work with abstractions reliably.

questions.q: What does the DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principle advocate?
options: Avoid writing any comments in code
Every piece of knowledge should be expressed in only one place
Every class should be as small as possible
Delete unused code immediately
questions.explanation: DRY means avoiding duplicated logic by extracting common patterns into reusable methods or classes. This reduces bugs and makes maintenance easier since changes happen in one place.

questions.q: If two classes share a similar method, what's a better approach than copying the method to both?
options: Keep the duplication to avoid complexity
Extract the common method to a base class or utility class and reuse it
Combine both classes into one mega-class
Duplicate the method and modify each copy slightly
questions.explanation: Extracting the common method applies DRY and reduces maintenance burden. Both classes can then inherit or delegate to the shared implementation.

questions.q: What negative impact does tight coupling between classes have?
options: It makes code faster to execute
Changing one class forces changes in tightly coupled classes, making refactoring risky and expensive
It reduces the number of interfaces needed
Tight coupling guarantees code correctness
questions.explanation: Tight coupling creates a ripple effect where changes in one class force cascading changes in dependencies. This makes code fragile and expensive to maintain.

questions.q: In a system where Service classes depend directly on concrete Database implementations, what design problem exists and how should it be fixed?
options: No problem; this is the standard approach in all systems
Service is tightly coupled to Database implementations; use dependency injection and interfaces to decouple them
Database should inherit from Service to fix the relationship
Service and Database should be combined into a single class
questions.explanation: Direct dependence on concrete implementations creates tight coupling. Using interfaces and dependency injection decouples Service from specific Database implementations.

questions.q: A MonsterFactory creates different monsters (Goblin, Orc, Dragon) with the same ability initialization logic. Why should this logic be centralized?
options: Centralization forces all monsters to be identical
Centralization applies DRY and ensures consistency; changes to initialization logic happen once, benefiting all monsters
Centralization makes code slower to execute
No advantage; each monster should have its own initialization logic
questions.explanation: Centralizing common logic in a factory reduces duplication, ensures consistency, and makes bugs easier to fix since the logic exists in one place.

questions.q: When designing a system, why should abstractions be stable while concrete implementations can change?
options: Abstractions are less important than implementations
Stable abstractions ensure clients don't break when implementations change; implementations can be swapped without affecting the interface contract
Concrete implementations are always more stable than abstractions
Both abstractions and implementations should change frequently
questions.explanation: A stable abstraction (interface) provides a contract that clients depend on. Implementations can be replaced, improved, or refactored without breaking clients that depend only on the abstraction.
